articles.title: FEATURES

articles.title: Read more on the Metronome blog
THE FIRST $100,000,000 ARR AT DATADOG
articles.summary: It's hard to imagine what real customer focus looks like before a company has revenue, but Datadog built it in from the start. The founders spent six months interviewing users before writing code, opened betas so customers could self-select, and sold month to month to spot churn immediately. That approach scaled to 700 employees and $100 million ARR. Engineers still rotate through support, pricing talks double as product feedback, and leadership treats every complaint as a signal. The result is a culture designed to find bad news fast and fix it before it compounds.

articles.title: FOUNDERS AND INVESTORS SLAM UK “EXIT TAX”
articles.summary: The UK government is considering a 20% “exit tax” on founders who build businesses in Britain but move abroad to avoid taxes. More than 150 entrepreneurs and investors - behind companies like Beamery, Cleo, and funds including Dawn Capital and 20VC - have pushed back, warning it would punish success and drive talent out. They say Britain already feels less competitive after capital gains hikes, and another tax like this could be the final push for many to leave.

articles.title: STARTUP OPERATING PRINCIPLES
articles.summary: Sam Gerstenzang grew from a fine to a good operator in three and a half years of working at Stripe. This post shares how Stripe approaches decision-making and communication. Operating well is a state. Peak performance is a constant process of tweaking, iterating, and tuning.

articles.title: STRATEGISM: MASTERING THE FOUNDER LETTER
articles.summary: In an era of formulaic marketing and AI-written content, the founder letter stands out as one of the few tools that still feels human. It works because it earns trust through story instead of shouting for attention - naming a problem, stirring emotion, and only then offering the solution. From Jason Fried's product launches to the plainspoken clarity of Jeff Bezos and Warren Buffett, the format proves that narrative still builds belief and conviction. Even small tests show it moves results: pages using a founder's voice convert higher because readers can tell when something was written by a person who actually means it.

articles.title: HOW TO ACTUALLY RAISE YOUR ROUND
articles.summary: Genomines' founder spent 14 months doing exactly the same thing: he pitched the same company over hundreds of conversations, facing the same objections and polite rejections. Then he closed $45 million from 12 funds. This post looks at what it really takes to raise a round. It's a messy and non-linear process.

articles.summary: Context is an AI-powered office suite that generates docs, slides, and spreadsheets using your data, tools, and preferences.

articles.title: THE COMPANY AS A MACHINE FOR DOING STUFF
articles.summary: Companies are machines for doing the stuff a founder really wants to do, but bigger. The company is a vehicle to accomplish a goal that would otherwise be impossible alone. Many founders have found wealth, yet they reinvest it to reach further. Many people work for the fruit - it is rare to find people who work for the work itself.

articles.title: THE COMPOUNDING STARTUP
articles.summary: An analysis of 6,525 software companies revealed that outliers and "normies" had nearly identical metrics at $1M ARR (the top 25% of failures actually grew faster than half the eventual winners). The difference emerged in relentless operational improvement: 86% of outliers increased expansion revenue by 10%+, 72% boosted ARPA significantly, and 51% improved retention - all while “normies” stayed mostly flat. The compound effect is staggering as a 50% improvement in pricing for new customers doubled annual growth rates from 11% to 24%, while combining better pricing and retention took growth "off the charts."

articles.title: SOFTWARE IS THE NEW ART FORM
articles.summary: Just as furniture, fashion, and food progressed from essentialist basics to art forms that make you feel something, software is speedrunning the same transformation. The frontier is now defined by software that either expands what's possible or makes you feel - and occasionally does both.

articles.title: COMPOUNDING OFFDEAL
articles.summary: Investment banking is a system that can be deconstructed, measured, and optimized, something software engineers are uniquely positioned to do exceptionally well.

articles.title: WHEN WORK STOPPED BEING A PLACE
articles.summary: Remote work gave us flexibility, but killed the "learning by osmosis" that happened when juniors could watch seniors read a room, make tough calls, or answer dumb questions during a coffee break.

articles.title: OPTIMIZING THE LANDING PAGE
articles.summary: Tweaking and obsessing over your conversion rates won't save you if the wrong people are showing up.
